nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving forest streams and swampy pools in dense tropical rainforest; water is heavily stained with tannins from leaf litter and decaying vegetation, resulting in very soft, acidic blackwater with minimal light penetration

decorPreferences: blackwater tannin staining (dried Indian almond leaves, alder cones, driftwood)
dark substrate (fine black sand or dark gravel)
dense aquatic planting with Microsorum, Bolbitis, or Java moss
floating plants to diffuse lighting (Salvinia, Pistia)
low, indirect lighting to replicate forest-floor conditions
leaf litter bed to provide cover and biofilm grazing sites

diet.notes: Micro-predator requiring live or frozen micro-foods for long-term health. Staple diet should include live or frozen Daphnia, Moina, baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), micro-worms, and grindal worms. Dry micro-pellets and crushed flake may be accepted by established fish but should not form the bulk of the diet. Refuses food when stressed and can be slow to acclimate to prepared foods. Feed small amounts 2–3 times daily.

goodWith: other small West/Central African micro-fish (Microdevario, Boraras)
small blackwater-adapted tetras (ember tetras, chocolate tetras)
dwarf Corydoras (C. hastatus, C. pygmaeus) in suitably soft water
small peaceful rasboras with overlapping water parameter requirements
avoidWith: hard-water fish incompatible with soft acid conditions
larger barbs or any boisterous fish that cause stress
aggressive or fast-moving species that out-compete for food
cichlids (except smallest shell-less species in appropriate water)
large shrimp that may predate on micro-foods before fish can eat

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Barbus hulstaerti (synonym). Verify availability on FishBase species page for Enteromius hulstaerti.

summary: The African Butterfly Barb (Enteromius hulstaerti) is a tiny nano fish from the Tshuapa River basin of the Democratic Republic of the Congo, reaching around 3 cm and recognisable by its vivid yellow flanks and bold black butterfly-shaped blotch. It demands very soft, acidic blackwater and live micro-foods, and is best suited to advanced keepers.
careNotes: The African Butterfly Barb is one of the more visually striking African nano barbs, its yellow flanks marked by a bold black butterfly-wing-shaped blotch. The species was formerly placed in the genus Barbus but is now recognised as Enteromius hulstaerti following a molecular phylogenetic revision of African cyprinids. It originates from the Tshuapa River basin in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, where it inhabits extremely soft, tannin-stained blackwater forest streams with negligible hardness and pH values frequently below 5.5. Replicating these conditions in the aquarium is essential: the target range is pH 4.5–6.5, gH 0–5, and kH as close to zero as practical. Reverse-osmosis or rain water softened further with tannin-releasing botanicals works well; hard or alkaline tap water tends to compromise immune function, suppress colour, and shorten lifespan noticeably. The tank should be heavily furnished with Indian almond leaves, alder cones, and driftwood to release natural tannins, paired with a dark substrate and floating plants to keep light intensity low. Filtration should be gentle — a sponge filter suits this species well, as it inhabits near-stagnant water in the wild. A minimum group size of 8 is recommended, and a group of 10–15 or more tends to produce natural shoaling behaviour and visibly reduces individual stress. Feed primarily live or frozen micro-foods such as Daphnia, Moina, baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), micro-worms, and grindal worms, offered 2–3 times daily. Dry foods are often accepted by settled fish but should supplement rather than replace live and frozen items. The African Butterfly Barb tends to be sensitive to new-tank syndrome and deteriorating water quality, so pristine, stable conditions are essential throughout its life.
breedingNotes: Breeding is possible but requires pristine soft, acidic water and thorough conditioning on live foods. The African Butterfly Barb is an egg scatterer that deposits eggs among fine-leaved plants or Java moss and provides no parental care; adults may consume eggs and fry, so a separate breeding tank is generally recommended. Condition a group of 6–10 well-fed adults, then move the best-coloured pairs or a small group to a densely planted breeding tank with pH 5.0–6.0, very soft water, and gentle filtration. Fry hatch within 36–48 hours and become free-swimming around day 3–4. First foods should be infusoria, paramecia, or commercial powdered fry food, graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii by the second week. Full fry-rearing success is considered moderate to difficult and requires close attention to micro-food availability and water stability.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Enteromius hulstaerti
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Tshuapa basin, DR Congo), water parameters (temperature 20–26°C, pH 4.0–6.5, hardness <36 mg/L CaCO3), adult size (males to ~2.5 cm, females slightly larger to ~3 cm), diet (micro-invertebrates, small live/frozen foods), compatibility, tank setup, and taxonomy notes including Barbus → Enteromius reclassification.

additional.name: FishBase — Enteromius hulstaerti
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cyprinidae, Cypriniformes), native range (Congo basin, DRC), adult size (to 3.5 cm TL), temperature range (22–26°C), and diet (invertivore). Synonym Barbus hulstaerti confirmed; current valid name Enteromius hulstaerti per Eschmeyer’s Catalog of Fishes.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Butterfly Barb Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade care guide confirming advanced care level due to soft acid water requirement, micro-food feeding demands, and sensitivity to parameter fluctuations. Recommended minimum group size of 6–8 and species-appropriate blackwater setup. Tank size guidance (10–20 gal / 40–75 L) and temperature 22–26°C cited.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — African Butterfly Barb species profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source confirming blackwater biotope requirements, Tshuapa basin origin, distinctive butterfly flank marking, small adult size (~3 cm), and insistence on live/frozen micro-foods for sustained health. Also noted the species is rarely seen in trade and commands specialist-level husbandry knowledge.

fish.breedingNotes: The African Butterfly Barb scatters eggs among fine vegetation and provides no parental care; adults may consume eggs, so a separate breeding tank with pH 5.0–6.0 and very soft water is generally recommended. Adults should be conditioned on live foods before spawning. Fry require infusoria or paramecia as first foods, progressing to Artemia nauplii as they grow. Rearing fry to maturity is considered moderate to difficult.
